blocks:
Ayahs

قٓ ۚ وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ٱلْمَجِيدِ (١)
Qaf. By the glorious Quran. (1)
بَلْ عَجِبُوٓا۟ أَن جَآءَهُم مُّنذِرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَقَالَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ هَـٰذَا شَىْءٌ عَجِيبٌ (٢)
Indeed they consider it odd that a warner from among themselves should have come to them. So the faithless say, ‘This is an odd thing.’ (2)
أَءِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا ۖ ذَٰلِكَ رَجْعٌۢ بَعِيدٌ (٣)
‘What! When we are dead and have become dust, [shall we be raised again]? That is a far-fetched return!’ (3)
قَدْ عَلِمْنَا مَا تَنقُصُ ٱلْأَرْضُ مِنْهُمْ ۖ وَعِندَنَا كِتَـٰبٌ حَفِيظٌۢ (٤)
We know what the earth diminishes from them, and with Us is a preserving Book. (4)
بَلْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ فَهُمْ فِىٓ أَمْرٍ مَّرِيجٍ (٥)
Indeed, they denied the truth when it came to them; so they are now in a perplexed state of affairs. (5)
أَفَلَمْ يَنظُرُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَوْقَهُمْ كَيْفَ بَنَيْنَـٰهَا وَزَيَّنَّـٰهَا وَمَا لَهَا مِن فُرُوجٍ (٦)
Have they not, then, observed the heaven above them, how We have built it and adorned it, and that there are no cracks in it? (6)
وَٱلْأَرْضَ مَدَدْنَـٰهَا وَأَلْقَيْنَا فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍۭ بَهِيجٍ (٧)
And We spread out the earth, and cast in it firm mountains, and caused every delightful kind [of vegetation] to grow in it. (7)
تَبْصِرَةً وَذِكْرَىٰ لِكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُّنِيبٍ (٨)
[In this there is] an insight and admonition for every penitent servant. (8)
وَنَزَّلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً مُّبَـٰرَكًا فَأَنۢبَتْنَا بِهِۦ جَنَّـٰتٍ وَحَبَّ ٱلْحَصِيدِ (٩)
We send down from the sky salubrious water, with which We grow gardens and the grain which is harvested, (9)
وَٱلنَّخْلَ بَاسِقَـٰتٍ لَّهَا طَلْعٌ نَّضِيدٌ (١٠)
and tall date palms with regularly set spathes, (10)
رِّزْقًا لِّلْعِبَادِ ۖ وَأَحْيَيْنَا بِهِۦ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْخُرُوجُ (١١)
as a provision for Our servants, and with it We revive a dead country. Likewise will be the rising [from the dead]. (11)
كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلرَّسِّ وَثَمُودُ (١٢)
The people of Noah denied before them, and [so did] the people of Rass and Thamud, (12)
وَعَادٌ وَفِرْعَوْنُ وَإِخْوَٰنُ لُوطٍ (١٣)
and ‘Ad, Pharaoh, and the brethren of Lot, (13)
وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأَيْكَةِ وَقَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ ۚ كُلٌّ كَذَّبَ ٱلرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ وَعِيدِ (١٤)
and the inhabitants of Aykah and the people of Tubbac. Each [of them] impugned the apostles, and so My threat became due [against them]. (14)

(Commentary)

The Surah mentions the Divine Call and points toward the warning contained therein regarding the Return (المعاد) — the restoration of life after death for final judgment. It addresses the polytheists' denial of it and their amazement at the notion that death entails the nullification of human personality (الشخصية الإنسانية) by becoming dust, leaving no trace of what one once was. They wonder how one could return a second time to the state they were in before death. The Surah counters the amazement and sense of impossibility they displayed by asserting that Divine Knowledge encompasses them, and with Him is the Preserved Book (الكتاب الحفيظ) from which nothing escapes, whether minute or grand, regarding the states of His creation. It then threatens them with an affliction similar to what befell the past perished nations.

Secondly, it draws attention to His knowledge and power, the Exalted, by pointing to His governance (تدبيره) in the creation of the heavens and how He adorned them with planets, stars, and other celestial bodies. It also points to the creation of the earth in terms of its expansion, the placing of firm mountains (الرواسي) upon it, and the growing of pairs of vegetation therein. This is followed by the sending down of water, the preparation of sustenance for the servants, and the reviving of the earth thereby.

Then, it provides an exposition of the state of man from the beginning of his creation, showing that he is under intense and precise surveillance—even regarding the words he utters and the thoughts that cross his mind or the whisperings of his soul (توسوس به نفسه) as long as he is alive. This continues until death overtakes him, then when he is resurrected for the Decisive Judgment (فصل القضاء), and finally when his reckoning is completed and he is entered into the Fire if he was among the deniers, or the Adorned Paradise if he was among the God-conscious (المتقين) — those who maintain an inner state of godwariness and protection against sin.

In short, the focal point of the discourse in the Surah is the Return (المعاد). Among the most luminous verses (غرر الآيات) within it are His sayings: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ "You were certainly oblivious of this. We have apprehended from you your veil, and so your sight is sharp today" (50:22); and يَوْمَ نَقُولُ لِجَهَنَّمَ هَلِ امْتَلَأْتِ وَتَقُولُ هَلْ مِنْ مَزِيدٍ "The day We shall say to hell, ‘Are you full?’ and it will say, ‘Is there any more?’" (50:30); and لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها وَلَدَيْنا مَزِيدٌ "There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more" (50:35).

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses, except for what has been claimed regarding His saying: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ "Certainly We created the heavens and the earth..." (50:38), whether one or two verses; however, there is no linguistic evidence to support this claim.

The verses we have cited contain a summary reference to the Return (المعاد) and their deeming it improbable, followed by a summary response and threat. This is then followed by a detailed exposition of the response and the threat.

His saying, the Exalted: ق وَالْقُرْآنِ الْمَجِيدِ "Qaf. By the glorious Quran" (50:1). It is stated in al-Majma‘: Glory (المجد) in their speech refers to expansive nobility (الشرف الواسع). It is said: "The man became glorious" (مجد الرجل) — using both the damma and fatha on the middle radical — when he becomes great and noble. Its origin is from their saying: "The camels became glorious" (مجدت الإبل) when their bellies become large from eating extensively of the spring herbage. End quote.

His saying: وَالْقُرْآنِ الْمَجِيدِ is an oath (قسم), and its response (جواب) is omitted, indicated by the subsequent sentences. The implied meaning is: "By the glorious Quran, the Resurrection is true," or "Indeed, you are among the warners," or "The warning is true." It has been said that the response to the oath is mentioned, being His saying: بَلْ عَجِبُوا "Rather they consider it odd..." (50:2). Others said it is His saying: قَدْ عَلِمْنا ما تَنْقُصُ "We know what the earth takes away..." (50:4). Others said it is: ما يَلْفِظُ مِنْ قَوْلٍ "He utters no word..." (50:18). Others said it is: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَذِكْرى "There is indeed a reminder in that..." (50:37). Others said it is: ما يُبَدَّلُ الْقَوْلُ لَدَيَ "The word is not altered with Me..." (50:29). These are weak opinions that should not be followed.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ عَجِبُوا أَنْ جاءَهُمْ مُنْذِرٌ مِنْهُمْ فَقالَ الْكافِرُونَ هذا شَيْءٌ عَجِيبٌ "Rather they consider it odd that a warner should have come to them from among themselves. So the faithless say, ‘This is an odd thing!’" (50:2). This is a digression (إضراب) from the content of the omitted response to the oath. It is as if it were said: "We sent you as a warner, yet they did not believe in you; rather, they wondered that a warner from among themselves came to them." Or it was said: "Indeed, the Resurrection about which you warned them is true, yet they did not believe in it; rather, they wondered at it and deemed it far-fetched."

The pronoun "among them" (منهم) in His saying: بَلْ عَجِبُوا أَنْ جاءَهُمْ مُنْذِرٌ مِنْهُمْ refers to them insofar as they are human beings—meaning from their own species. This is because the idolaters used to deny the prophethood of humans, as has been pointed out repeatedly. Or it refers to them insofar as they are Arabs, meaning: "Rather, they wondered that a warner from their own people and in their own tongue came to them, clarifying the truth to them in the most complete manner," which would be more profound in rebuking them.

His saying: فَقالَ الْكافِرُونَ هذا شَيْءٌ عَجِيبٌ describes them as "disbelievers" (الكافرون) rather than saying "the polytheists said" or similar terms, to indicate their concealment (ستر) of the truth when it came to them. The demonstrative "this" (هذا) in their saying: "This is an odd thing" refers to the Resurrection and the return to Allah, as explained by the subsequent verse: أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً "What! When we are dead and have become dust?..." (50:3).

His saying, the Exalted: أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً ذلِكَ رَجْعٌ بَعِيدٌ "What! When we are dead and have become dust? That is a far-fetched return!" (50:3). Return (الرجع) and ruju‘ (الرجوع) carry the same meaning. What is meant by "far-fetched" (بعيد) is that which is distant from the intellect (العقل).

The response to "when" (إذا) in their saying: أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً is omitted, indicated by their saying: ذلِكَ رَجْعٌ بَعِيدٌ. The implied meaning is: "When we are dead and have become dust, shall we be resurrected and returned?" The interrogative is for expressing amazement. It was omitted to indicate that it is so strange that it ought not even be mentioned, as the intellect of any rational person would not accept it.

The verse follows the same course as His saying: وَقالُوا أَإِذا ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ "They say, ‘When we have been lost in the earth, shall we indeed be [re-created] in a new creation?’" (32:10).

The meaning is that they wonder and say: "When we have died and become dust—and our essences have been nullified such that no trace of them remains—shall we be resurrected and returned?" Then, as if someone were to ask them: "At what do you wonder?" they replied: "That is a far-fetched return which the intellect deems remote and does not concede."

His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ عَلِمْنا ما تَنْقُصُ الْأَرْضُ مِنْهُمْ وَعِنْدَنا كِتابٌ حَفِيظٌ "We know what the earth takes away from them, and with Us is a preserved Book" (50:4). This is a rebuttal from Him, the Exalted, to their deeming the Resurrection and Return far-fetched, based on their argument that their bodies will disintegrate upon death and become dust with indistinguishable parts, where no part can be differentiated from another. The response is: "We know what the earth consumes of their bodies and what it diminishes from them; thus, not a single part of their components escapes Our knowledge such that its restoration would be difficult for Us or impossible due to ignorance."

Or it means: "We know who among them dies and is buried in the earth, so the earth diminishes them from their collective number." In the first interpretation, "from" (من) is partitive (تبعيضية), and in the second, it is explanatory (تبيينية).

His saying: وَعِنْدَنا كِتابٌ حَفِيظٌ means a book that preserves (حافظ) everything, its traces, and its states; or a book that records events and is protected (محفوظ) from alteration and tampering. This is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) — the celestial record containing the archetypal reality of all things — which contains everything that was, is, and will be until the Day of Resurrection.

The claim of some that it refers to the "Book of Deeds" (كتاب الأعمال) is incorrect. Firstly, because Allah mentioned it as preserving what the earth diminishes from them, which is different from the deeds preserved by the Book of Deeds. Secondly, He, the Glorified, only described the Preserved Tablet with the attribute of "preservation" (الحفظ) in His speech, not the Books of Deeds; thus, interpreting the "Preserved Book" as the Book of Deeds is without evidence.

The summary of the verse's response is that they presumed their death and transformation into dust with disintegrated atoms and indistinguishable parts would make their components unknown to Us, rendering their gathering and restoration impossible. However, this is a false presumption, for We know who has died among them, what parts of their bodies turn into earth, how they change, and where they end up. And with Us is a Preserved Book containing everything, which is the Preserved Tablet.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِالْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ فَهُمْ فِي أَمْرٍ مَرِيجٍ "Rather they denied the truth when it came to them; so they are in a confused state" (50:5). Confusion (المرج) is mixing and ambiguity. The verse contains a digression (إضراب) from what the previous verse suggested. What was suggested was that they only wondered at the matter of Resurrection and Return and deemed it far-fetched due to their ignorance that Allah, the Glorified, is All-Knowing, and that nothing of the states of His creation or their traces escapes Him, and that all of that is inscribed in the Preserved Tablet with Allah such that nothing deviates from it.

He digresses in this verse to state that this is not due to their ignorance—even if they feign ignorance—but rather they denied the truth when it came to them and it became clear to them that it is the truth. Thus, they are deniers (جاحدون) of the truth and obstinate (معاندون) toward it; they are not ignorant of it or incapable of perceiving it. Consequently, they are in a "confused state" (أمر مريج)—mixed and disordered—perceiving the truth yet denying it, despite the fact that the necessary consequence of knowing something is to affirm it and believe in it.

It has been said: The meaning of their being in a "confused state" is that they are bewildered after denying the truth, not knowing what to say. Sometimes they say: "A fabrication against Allah," sometimes: "Sorcery," sometimes: "Poetry," sometimes: "Soothsaying," and sometimes: "Divination."

For this reason, the discourse is followed by the mention of the signs of His knowledge and power as a rebuke to them, and then by a reference to the denial of the past perished nations which led them to the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال), as a threat to them.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَلَمْ يَنْظُرُوا إِلَى السَّماءِ فَوْقَهُمْ كَيْفَ بَنَيْناها وَزَيَّنَّاها وَما لَها مِنْ فُرُوجٍ "Have they not observed the sky above them, how We have fashioned it and adorned it, and it has no cracks?" (50:6). Cracks (الفروج) is the plural of furjah: splits and openings. Restricting the sky to being "above them" indicates that it is within their sight and does not disappear from their vision. What is meant by "adorning it" is the creation of the shining stars within it with their exquisite beauty. Thus, the construction of this marvelous creation with its stunning beauty, without splits or openings, is the truest witness to His overwhelming power and His encompassing knowledge of what He created.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْأَرْضَ مَدَدْناها وَأَلْقَيْنا فِيها رَواسِيَ وَأَنْبَتْنا فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ بَهِيجٍ "And the earth, We have spread it out, and cast in it firm mountains, and We have grown in it every kind of delightful pair" (50:7). Spreading the earth (مد الأرض) is its expansion to suit human life. Firm mountains (الرواسي) is the plural of rasiyah, meaning stable; it is an adjective whose noun—the mountains—is omitted. What is meant is placing stable mountains upon its surface. Delightful (البهيج) comes from bahjah (delight). It is stated in al-Majma‘: Delight is the beauty that possesses splendor upon being seen, like flowers, lush trees, and green meadows. End quote. It has also been said: What is meant by "delightful" is that which delights and pleases whoever sees it; thus, it carries the meaning of "that which causes delight."

What is meant by growing "every delightful pair" is the growing of every category of plant that is beautiful in appearance.

Thus, the creation of the earth and the marvelous Divine governance that occurs within it is the best evidence that guides the intellect toward the perfection of power and knowledge.

His saying, the Exalted: تَبْصِرَةً وَذِكْرى لِكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُنِيبٍ "As an insight and a reminder for every penitent servant" (50:8). This is an object of purpose (مفعول له), meaning: We did what We did of constructing the sky, spreading the earth, and the wonders of governance We conducted in them, so that it may be an insight (تبصرة) by which one gains vision and a reminder (ذكرى) by which every servant returning (منيب) to Allah, the Glorified, may remember.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنَزَّلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً مُبارَكاً فَأَنْبَتْنا بِهِ جَنَّاتٍ وَحَبَّ الْحَصِيدِ "And We sent down from the sky blessed water, and We grew with it gardens and the harvest grain" (50:9). The sky (السماء) refers to the direction of height, and the blessed water is rain. It is described as "blessed" (مبارك) due to the abundance of its benefits returning to the earth and its inhabitants. The harvest grain (حب الحصيد) refers to the grain that is harvested; this is an instance of adding the noun to its adjective. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالنَّخْلَ باسِقاتٍ لَها طَلْعٌ نَضِيدٌ "And tall palm trees with clustered spathes" (50:10). Tall (الباسقات) is the plural of basiqah, which means long and high. Spathe (الطلع) is the first thing that emerges from the fruit of the palm trees. Clustered (النضيد) carries the meaning of being arranged (منضود) one upon another. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: رِزْقاً لِلْعِبادِ وَأَحْيَيْنا بِهِ بَلْدَةً مَيْتاً كَذلِكَ الْخُرُوجُ "As a provision for the servants, and with it We revive a dead land. Likewise is the resurrection" (50:11). Provision (الرزق) is that which sustains existence. As a provision for the servants (رِزْقاً لِلْعِبادِ) is an object of purpose, meaning: We grew these gardens, the harvest grain, and the tall palm trees with their clustered spathes to be a provision for the servants. Thus, He who created these plants to provide for the servants—with all the vast governance involved that astounds the mind and bewilders the intellect—is the Possessor of infinite knowledge and tireless power. It is not difficult for Him to revive man after his death, even if the atoms of his body have disintegrated and the parts of his frame have been lost in the earth.

His saying: وَأَحْيَيْنا بِهِ بَلْدَةً مَيْتاً كَذلِكَ الْخُرُوجُ is another proof for the Resurrection, distinct from what preceded, concluded from the flow of the discourse. The previous exposition in rebutting their deeming the Resurrection far-fetched—based on their becoming dust with indistinguishable parts—was a proof from the path of establishing His knowledge of everything and His power over everything. This proof, contained in His saying: "And with it We revive a dead land. Likewise is the resurrection," is from the path of establishing the possibility of a thing by the occurrence of its like. The "coming out" (الخروج) from the graves through revival after death is nothing but like the coming out of dead vegetation from the earth after its death and the cessation of its powers of growth and development.

We have established this proof in the commentary on verses that use the revival of the earth after its death as evidence for the Resurrection multiple times in previous parts of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ "The people of Noah denied before them..." until His saying: كُلٌّ كَذَّبَ الرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ وَعِيدِ "...each denied the apostles, so My threat came true" (50:12-14). This is a threat and a warning to them because they denied the truth when it came to them and it became clear to them, out of obstinacy, as we pointed out before.

The mention of the "People of the Well" (أصحاب الرس) has already passed in the commentary of Surah al-Furqan; the mention of the "People of the Thicket" (أصحاب الأيكة), who are the people of Shu‘ayb, in Surahs al-Hijr, al-Shu‘ara, and Sad; and the mention of the "People of Tubba‘" in Surah al-Dukhan.

In His saying: كُلٌّ كَذَّبَ الرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ وَعِيدِ there is an indication that there is a threat of destruction that is fulfilled upon the denial of the messengers. The Exalted said: فَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ "So travel over the land and observe how was the fate of the deniers" (16:36).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "Allah, the Exalted, created behind this earth a sea encompassing it. Then He created behind that a mountain called Qaf, over which the lower heaven flutters. Then He created behind that mountain an earth—seven times like that earth. Then He created behind that a sea encompassing it. Then He created behind that a mountain called Qaf, over which the second heaven flutters," until he counted seven earths, seven seas, seven mountains, and seven heavens. He said: "And that is His saying: وَالْبَحْرُ يَمُدُّهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ سَبْعَةُ أَبْحُرٍ '...and the sea were replenished by seven more seas...'" (31:27).

In the same source, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Mardawayh, Abu al-Shaykh, and al-Hakim narrated from Abdullah ibn Buraydah regarding His saying, the Exalted: ق (Qaf). He said: "A mountain of emerald encompassing the world, upon which the edges of the heaven rest."

In the same source, Ibn Abi al-Dunya narrated in al-‘Uqubat, and Abu al-Shaykh in al-‘Azamah, from Ibn Abbas who said: "Allah created a mountain called Qaf encompassing the world, and its roots reach the Rock upon which the earth rests. When Allah intends to cause an earthquake in a village, He commands that mountain, and it moves the root adjacent to that village, causing it to quake and move. Thus, one village moves while another does not." I say: al-Qummi narrated with his chain from Yahya ibn Maysarah al-Khath‘ami from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) something similar to what was passed from Abdullah ibn Buraydah. What carries this meaning has also been narrated in a mursal (مرسلاً) — a narration where the link to the Prophet or Imam is missing — and mudmar (مضمراً) — a narration where the speaker is not explicitly named — form, and its wording is: "He said: A mountain encompassing the world behind Yajuj and Majuj."

Regardless, these narrations are not to be relied upon. The falsehood of what they contain has almost reached the level of self-evident truths (البديهيات) today, or indeed it is among them.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَقالَ الْكافِرُونَ هذا شَيْءٌ عَجِيبٌ "So the faithless say, ‘This is an odd thing!’" (50:2). He said: "It was revealed concerning Ubayy ibn Khalaf. He said to Abu Jahl: 'Come to me, I will show you something amazing from Muhammad.' Then he took a bone and crumbled it, then said: 'O Muhammad, do you claim that this will live?' So Allah revealed: بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِالْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ فَهُمْ فِي أَمْرٍ مَرِيجٍ 'Rather they denied the truth when it came to them; so they are in a confused state'" (50:5).

Ayahs

أَفَعَيِينَا بِٱلْخَلْقِ ٱلْأَوَّلِ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ فِى لَبْسٍ مِّنْ خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ (١٥)
Have We been exhausted by the first creation? No, they are in doubt about a new creation. (15)
وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ وَنَعْلَمُ مَا تُوَسْوِسُ بِهِۦ نَفْسُهُۥ ۖ وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ ٱلْوَرِيدِ (١٦)
Certainly We have created man and We know to what his soul tempts him, and We are nearer to him than his jugular vein. (16)
إِذْ يَتَلَقَّى ٱلْمُتَلَقِّيَانِ عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ ٱلشِّمَالِ قَعِيدٌ (١٧)
When the twin recorders record [his deeds], seated on the right hand and on the left: (17)
مَّا يَلْفِظُ مِن قَوْلٍ إِلَّا لَدَيْهِ رَقِيبٌ عَتِيدٌ (١٨)
he says no word but that there is a ready observer beside him. (18)
وَجَآءَتْ سَكْرَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ مَا كُنتَ مِنْهُ تَحِيدُ (١٩)
The throes of death bring the truth: ‘This is what you used to shun!’ (19)
وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلْوَعِيدِ (٢٠)
And the Trumpet will be blown: ‘This is the promised day.’ (20)
وَجَآءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّعَهَا سَآئِقٌ وَشَهِيدٌ (٢١)
Every soul will come accompanied by [two angels], a driver, and a witness: (21)
لَّقَدْ كُنتَ فِى غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا فَكَشَفْنَا عَنكَ غِطَآءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ (٢٢)
[he will be told] ‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so today your eyesight is acute.’ (22)
وَقَالَ قَرِينُهُۥ هَـٰذَا مَا لَدَىَّ عَتِيدٌ (٢٣)
Then his companion [angel] will say, ‘This is what is ready with me [of his record of deeds].’ (23)
أَلْقِيَا فِى جَهَنَّمَ كُلَّ كَفَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ (٢٤)
[The two angels accompany him will be told,] ‘Cast every obdurate ingrate into hell, (24)
مَّنَّاعٍ لِّلْخَيْرِ مُعْتَدٍ مُّرِيبٍ (٢٥)
[every] hinderer of good, transgressor, and skeptic, (25)
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَأَلْقِيَاهُ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلشَّدِيدِ (٢٦)
who had set up another god along with Allah and cast him into the severe punishment.’ (26)
۞ قَالَ قَرِينُهُۥ رَبَّنَا مَآ أَطْغَيْتُهُۥ وَلَـٰكِن كَانَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (٢٧)
His companion [devil] will say, ‘Our Lord! I did not incite him to rebel [against You], but he was [himself] in extreme error.’ (27)
قَالَ لَا تَخْتَصِمُوا۟ لَدَىَّ وَقَدْ قَدَّمْتُ إِلَيْكُم بِٱلْوَعِيدِ (٢٨)
He will say, ‘Do not wrangle in My presence, for I had already warned you in advance. (28)
مَا يُبَدَّلُ ٱلْقَوْلُ لَدَىَّ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ (٢٩)
The word [of judgement] is unalterable with Me, and I am not tyrannical to My servants.’ (29)
يَوْمَ نَقُولُ لِجَهَنَّمَ هَلِ ٱمْتَلَأْتِ وَتَقُولُ هَلْ مِن مَّزِيدٍ (٣٠)
The day when We shall say to hell, ‘Are you full?’ It will say, ‘Is there any more?’ (30)
وَأُزْلِفَتِ ٱلْجَنَّةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ (٣١)
And paradise will be brought near for the Godwary, it will not be distant [any more]: (31)
هَـٰذَا مَا تُوعَدُونَ لِكُلِّ أَوَّابٍ حَفِيظٍ (٣٢)
‘This is what you were promised. [It is] for every penitent and dutiful [servant] (32)
مَّنْ خَشِىَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَجَآءَ بِقَلْبٍ مُّنِيبٍ (٣٣)
who fears the All-beneficent in secret and comes with a penitent heart. (33)
ٱدْخُلُوهَا بِسَلَـٰمٍ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلْخُلُودِ (٣٤)
Enter it in peace! This is the day of immortality.’ (34)
لَهُم مَّا يَشَآءُونَ فِيهَا وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ (٣٥)
There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more. (35)
وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْنٍ هُمْ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُم بَطْشًا فَنَقَّبُوا۟ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ هَلْ مِن مَّحِيصٍ (٣٦)
How many generations We have destroyed before them, who were stronger than these, insomuch that they ransacked the towns?! So, is there any escape [from Allah’s punishment]? (36)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَذِكْرَىٰ لِمَن كَانَ لَهُۥ قَلْبٌ أَوْ أَلْقَى ٱلسَّمْعَ وَهُوَ شَهِيدٌ (٣٧)
There is indeed an admonition in that for one who has a heart, or gives ear, being attentive. (37)
وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ وَمَا مَسَّنَا مِن لُّغُوبٍ (٣٨)
Certainly We created the heavens and the earth, and whatever is between them, in six days, and any fatigue did not touch Us. (38)

(Commentary)

The first verse completes the argument presented in the preceding verses regarding His knowledge and power, evidenced by His creation of the heavens and the earth and all that is within them—a creation He initiated and governed with the most perfect and complete management (التدبير). All of this constitutes the first creation (الخلق الأول) and the first inception (النشأة الأولى). He concludes this by saying: أَفَعَيِينا بِالْخَلْقِ الْأَوَّلِ "Were We then fatigued by the first creation?" (50:15). From this, He deduces that the One capable of the first creation and knowledgeable of it is likewise capable of a new creation (خلق جديد) and a second inception (نشأة ثانية), and is knowledgeable of it as well. This is because the two are similar; if the creation of one is possible for Him, the creation of the other is also possible. Indeed, His command, when He wills a thing, is only that He says to it, "Be," and it is.

Then, He turns away from this to state that they are in confusion (التباس) regarding a new creation, despite the similarity between the two creations. He then points to the first inception of man, noting that He knows even the whisperings of his heart, while his guardians watch over him with the most meticulous observation. Then comes the stupor of death (سكرة الموت) with the truth, followed by the Resurrection (البعث), and then entry into Paradise or the Fire. He then points a second time to what befell past generations who denied the truth—the divine wrath and the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال)—even though they were more powerful in might than these people. Thus, He who requited them with destruction is capable of requiting these people as well.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَعَيِينا بِالْخَلْقِ الْأَوَّلِ بَلْ هُمْ فِي لَبْسٍ مِنْ خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ "Were We then fatigued by the first creation? Rather they are in confusion about a new creation" (50:15). Fatigue (العي) is an incapacity (عجز) that afflicts one who undertakes a task or speech. Al-Raghib said: "It is said: 'Such a thing fatigued me' (أعياني) and 'I was fatigued by such a thing' (عييت), meaning I was incapable of it." The first creation (الخلق الأول) is the creation of this natural inception (النشأة الطبيعية) with its current system, including man in his worldly life. There is no reason to restrict the "first creation" to the creation of the heavens and the earth only, as al-Razi inclined toward in al-Tafsir al-Kabir, nor to restrict it to the creation of man, as some others have. This is because the "new creation" encompasses the heavens, the earth, and man altogether, as the Exalted says: يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ وَالسَّماواتُ وَبَرَزُوا لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ "The day when the earth is transformed into another earth and the heavens [as well], and they emerge before Allah, the One, the All-paramount" (14:48). The new creation (الخلق الجديد) is the creation of the second inception, which is the life of the Hereafter (النشأة الآخرة). The interrogative here is for negation (الإنكار).

The meaning is: Were We incapable of the first creation such that We would be incapable of the new creation? That is, We were not incapable of the first creation, which was its initiation (إبداؤه), so We shall not be incapable of the new creation, which is its restoration (إعادته).

If fatigue (العي) is taken to mean tiredness (التعب), as some have inclined, the meaning would be: Did We become tired because of the first creation such that the new creation becomes impossible or difficult for Us? This is similar to how a human or any other animal, when performing an action and doing much of it, ends up in physical exhaustion (التعب البدني), which prevents them from further action. Thus, the action they do not perform because they are tired is like the action they did perform, but it is not carried out because the agent lacks the strength due to exhaustion, even if the action itself is possible and similar to its counterparts.

This is a valid meaning, but it has been said that using fatigue (العي) to mean incapacity (العجز) is more eloquent.

Furthermore, framing the argument through the lens of incapacity (العجز) implies the impossibility (استحالة) of the act, and its negation is what is sought. This differs from framing it through tiredness (التعب), which implies difficulty (تعسر) rather than impossibility. Those who deny the Resurrection (المعاد) intend to claim its impossibility, not its mere difficulty.

His saying: بَلْ هُمْ فِي لَبْسٍ مِنْ خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ "Rather they are in confusion about a new creation" (50:15). Confusion (اللبس) is ambiguity (الالتباس). What is meant by the new creation (الخلق الجديد) is the transformation of their worldly inception into another inception with a different system beyond the natural system (النظام الطبيعي) governing this world. For in the other inception—which is the new creation—there is subsistence without annihilation and life without death. Furthermore, if the human is among the folk of felicity (أهل السعادة), he shall have blessing without misfortune; and if he is among the folk of wretchedness (أهل الشقاء), he shall be in misfortune with no blessing accompanying it. The first inception (النشأة الأولى)—which is the first creation—and the system governing it are contrary to that.

The meaning is: Since We created the world with its heavens, its earth, and all that is in them, and We managed it with the best management for the first time by Our power and knowledge, and We were not incapable of that in terms of knowledge or power, then We are not incapable of renewing its creation—which is transforming it into a new creation. Thus, there is no doubt regarding Our power, nor any ambiguity; rather, they are in a state of confusion (التباس) such that they find no way to believe in a new creation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ وَنَعْلَمُ ما تُوَسْوِسُ بِهِ نَفْسُهُ وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ الْوَرِيدِ "Certainly We have created man and We know what his soul tempts him with, and We are nearer to him than his jugular vein" (50:16). Al-Raghib said: "Whispering (الوسوسة) refers to an evil thought (الخطرة الرديئة), and its root is from al-waswas, which is the sound of jewelry or a faint whisper."

What is meant by the creation of man (خلق الإنسان) is his gradual and transforming existence, creation after creation, not just the beginning of his formation as a human, even though it is expressed in the past tense: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ "Certainly We have created man." This is because man—and indeed every creature that has a share of subsistence—just as he requires the gift of his Lord at the beginning of his existence, he likewise requires Him for his subsistence.

Because of this subtle point, He conjoined His saying: وَنَعْلَمُ ما تُوَسْوِسُ بِهِ نَفْسُهُ "and We know what his soul tempts him with"—which is a present-tense verb intended to denote continuity (الاستمرار)—to His saying: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ "Certainly We have created man"—which is a past-tense verb but continuous in meaning. Similarly, His saying: وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ الْوَرِيدِ "and We are nearer to him than his jugular vein" denotes fixity, permanence, and continuity alongside the continuity of man's existence.

The verse is connected to the preceding argument for His knowledge and power in the first creation in His saying: أَفَلَمْ يَنْظُرُوا إِلَى السَّماءِ "Have they not looked at the sky..." (50:6). It is also connected to His saying in the previous verse: بَلْ هُمْ فِي لَبْسٍ مِنْ خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ "Rather they are in confusion about a new creation." Thus, it is within a context that mentions His power over man through his creation, and His knowledge of him both directly and through the intermediary of the guardian angels who record deeds.

So His saying: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ "Certainly We have created man"—where the lam is for an oath—indicates His power over him by affirming the act of creation.

His saying: وَنَعْلَمُ ما تُوَسْوِسُ بِهِ نَفْسُهُ "and We know what his soul tempts him with" mentions the most hidden types of knowledge—knowledge of the subtle internal thoughts (الخطور النفساني)—as an indication of the comprehensiveness of His knowledge. It is as if it were said: "We know his manifest and his hidden, even what his soul whispers to him." Among the things the soul whispers is the doubt regarding the Resurrection (المعاد): "How can man be resurrected when, after death, he has become dust with scattered parts, indistinguishable from one another?"

It has become clear that the word "what" (ما) in ما تُوَسْوِسُ بِهِ is a relative pronoun, and the pronoun in بِهِ refers back to it, with the ba denoting the instrument or causality. The whispering is attributed to the soul (النفس) rather than the Devil (الشيطان)—even though it is also attributed to him—because the discourse concerns the encompassment of knowledge over man, even down to the fleeting thoughts and whispers in the corners of his soul.

His saying: وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ الْوَرِيدِ "and We are nearer to him than his jugular vein." The vein (الوريد) is a vessel dispersed throughout the body through which blood flows. It is also said to be the vein in the neck. In any case, it is called a "cord" (حبل) due to its resemblance to one, and the genitive construction "cord of the vein" (حبل الوريد) is explanatory (بيانية).

The meaning is: We are nearer to man than his jugular vein, which is mingled with his limbs and settled within his body; so how could We not know him and what is in his soul?

This is a simplification of the intended meaning through a straightforward sentence that is easily grasped by the general understanding. Otherwise, the matter of His nearness to him is far greater than that. For He, the Glorified, is the One who made it a soul and arranged its effects upon it. He is the intermediary between the soul and itself, and between it and its effects and actions. Thus, He is nearer to man than any conceivable thing, even his own self. Because this meaning is subtle (دقيق) and difficult for most minds to conceptualize, He, the Glorified, turned to explaining it in the manner of His saying: وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ الْوَرِيدِ. Similar to this in a certain respect is His saying: أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ "...that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart" (8:24).

Commentators have many other views regarding the meaning of this verse, but there is no benefit in quoting or discussing them; whoever desires them may refer to their books.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ يَتَلَقَّى الْمُتَلَقِّيانِ عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ الشِّمالِ قَعِيدٌ "When the two receivers receive [him], seated on the right and on the left" (50:17). Receiving (التلقي) means taking and learning. What is meant by the "two receivers" (المتلقيان), as indicated by the context, are the two angels entrusted with the human, who receive his deeds and preserve them by writing.

His saying: عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ الشِّمالِ قَعِيدٌ "seated on the right and on the left" implies: "on the right is a seated one, and on the left is a seated one." What is meant by the right and the left are the right and left sides of the human. The "seated one" (القعيد) is the one who sits (القاعد).

The adverbial phrase (الظرف) in His saying: إِذْ يَتَلَقَّى الْمُتَلَقِّيانِ "When the two receivers receive" is apparently connected to a deleted verb, the estimation being: "Remember when the two receivers receive." The intent is to point to His knowledge of man's deeds through the record of deeds (كتاب الأعمال) by the angels, in addition to His knowledge of his essence without the mediation of intermediaries.

It has also been said that the adverbial phrase is connected to the word "nearer" (أَقْرَبُ) in the previous verse. The meaning would then be: "We are nearer to him than his jugular vein at the time when the two angels entrusted with him receive his deeds to record them."

Perhaps the previous view is more in accord with the context, for this latter view is based on the premise that the primary purpose is to explain His nearness to him and His knowledge of him, with the rest being intended for that sake. However, the manifest context—especially considering the following verse—suggests that both the knowledge through nearness and the knowledge through the reception of the two angels are intended independently.

It has also been said that "when" (إِذْ) is causal (تعليلية), providing the reason for His knowledge indicated by His saying: وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ "and We are nearer to him...", by means of what follows it.

This is problematic because it is far from the style of the Quran to use the knowledge of angels or their preservation and recording as evidence for the knowledge of Allah.

His saying: عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ الشِّمالِ قَعِيدٌ "seated on the right and on the left" is a representation (تمثيل) of their position relative to the human. The right and left are the sides of good and evil, to which good deeds (الحسنة) and evil deeds (السيئة) are attributed.

His saying, the Exalted: ما يَلْفِظُ مِنْ قَوْلٍ إِلَّا لَدَيْهِ رَقِيبٌ عَتِيدٌ "He utters no word but that there is with him a vigilant observer, ready" (50:18). Uttering (اللفظ) means throwing; it is used for speaking by way of a kind of metaphor. The observer (الرقيب) is the guardian (المحافظ), and the ready one (العتيد) is the one prepared and poised to attend to the matter.

The verse mentions the monitoring of the human by the scribes regarding the words he utters. Coming after His saying: إِذْ يَتَلَقَّى الْمُتَلَقِّيانِ, it is a mention of the specific after the general due to the increased importance given to it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجاءَتْ سَكْرَةُ الْمَوْتِ بِالْحَقِّ ذلِكَ ما كُنْتَ مِنْهُ تَحِيدُ "The stupor of death brings the truth: ‘This is what you used to shun!’" (50:19). Shunning (الحيد) is turning away and deviating by way of flight. What is meant by the stupor of death (سكرة الموت) is what afflicts the human during the throes of death, as he becomes occupied with himself and cut off from people, like a drunkard (السكران) who knows not what he says nor what is said to him.

In qualifying the coming of the stupor of death with "the truth" (بالحق), there is an indication that death is included within the divine decree (القضاء الإلهي) and is intended for its own sake in the system of the universe (نظام الكون), as inferred from His saying: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً وَإِلَيْنا تُرْجَعُونَ "Every soul shall taste death, and We will test you with good and ill by way of trial, and to Us you will be brought back" (21:35), the commentary of which has already passed. Thus, death—which is the transition from this abode to the abode after it—is truth (حق), just as the Resurrection is truth, Paradise is truth, and the Fire is truth. There are other views regarding the meaning of death being "with the truth," but there is no benefit in quoting or addressing them.

In His saying: ذلِكَ ما كُنْتَ مِنْهُ تَحِيدُ "This is what you used to shun!" there is an indication that man naturally dislikes death. This is because Allah, the Glorified, has beautified the worldly life and attachment to its ornaments for man as a trial and a test. He says: إِنَّا جَعَلْنا ما عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَها لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً وَإِنَّا لَجاعِلُونَ ما عَلَيْها صَعِيداً جُرُزاً "Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct. And We will indeed turn whatever is on it into a barren plain" (18:7-8).

His saying, the Exalted: وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ ذلِكَ يَوْمُ الْوَعِيدِ "And the Trumpet will be blown: ‘This is the promised day!’" (50:20). This is a second transition to the world of eternity (عالم الخلود) by the blowing of the Trumpet after the first transition. What is meant by the blowing of the Trumpet is the second blast that establishes the Hour, or both blasts together by intending the absolute act of blowing.

The "promised day" (يوم الوعيد) refers to the Day of Resurrection, on which Allah fulfills His threat (وعيد) against the criminals among His servants.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجاءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَعَها سائِقٌ وَشَهِيدٌ "Every soul will come accompanied by a driver and a witness" (50:21). Driving (السياقة) is urging cattle to move from behind, as opposed to leading (القيادة), which is pulling them from the front.

So His saying: وَجاءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ "Every soul will come" means it comes to Allah and is present before Him for the final judgment (فصل القضاء). The evidence for this is His saying: إِلى رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْمَساقُ "To your Lord on that day will be the drive" (75:30).

The meaning is: Every soul shall be present before Him, the Exalted, accompanied by a driver who drives it and a witness who testifies to its deeds. He, the Exalted, did not explicitly state whether they are from the angels, or whether they are the two scribes, or from other than the angels. However, what first comes to mind from the context of the verses is that they are from the angels, and narrations regarding this will follow.

Likewise, there is no explicit statement that testimony is restricted to this witness mentioned in the verse; rather, the verses concerning the witnesses of the Day of Resurrection necessitate that it is not restricted. Similarly, the following verses mentioning the disputation of man provide a context indicating that there are others with man on that day besides the driver and the witness.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ "‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is sharp today’" (50:22). The occurrence of this verse within the context of the verses of the Resurrection and its being surrounded by them necessitates that it is among the discourses of the Day of Resurrection. The speaker is Allah, the Glorified, and the one addressed is the human mentioned in His saying: وَجاءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ. Accordingly, the address is general, directed to every human, although the reproach and rebuke (التوبيخ والتقريع) apparent from the context may require that the address be specific to those who deny the Resurrection. Additionally, the verses are framed to refute the deniers of the Resurrection in their saying: أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً ذلِكَ رَجْعٌ بَعِيدٌ "‘What! When we are dead and have become dust? That is a far-fetched return!’" (50:3).

The demonstration (الإشارة) in His saying "this" (هذا) refers to what he witnesses on that day and beholds of the severing of causes (تقطع الأسباب), the perishing of things, and the return of all to Allah, the One, the All-paramount. Man's attachment in the world to apparent causes and his reliance upon them had made him oblivious (أغفله) to this, until Allah removed from him the veil of obliviousness (حجاب الغفلة), and the reality of the matter appeared to him. Thus, he witnessed it with ocular vision (مشاهدة عيان), not merely through conceptual knowledge (علما فكريا).

Therefore, he is addressed with: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ "You were certainly" in the world فِي غَفْلَةٍ "oblivious"—which surrounded you—مِنْ هذا "of this" which you now witness and behold, even though in the world it was before your very eyes and never absent; but your attachment to the hem of causes distracted you and made you oblivious to it. فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ "We have removed your veil from you" today, فَبَصَرُكَ "and so your sight"—which is the insight (البصيرة) and the eye of the heart—الْيَوْمَ "today"—which is the Day of Resurrection—حَدِيدٌ "is sharp," meaning penetrating, seeing what it could not see in the world.

From this verse, it becomes clear, firstly: that the definition of the Day of Resurrection is that it is a day in which the veil of obliviousness is removed from man, and he witnesses the reality of the matter. Regarding this meaning and what is close to it, there are many verses, such as His saying: وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ "...and on that day all command will belong to Allah" (82:19), and His saying: لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ "‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’" (40:16), among other verses.

Secondly: that what man witnesses on the Day of Resurrection is already present and prepared for him while he is in the world, except that he is oblivious to it. The specific characteristic of the Day of Resurrection is that it is the day of the removal of the veil and the beholding of what lies behind it. This is because obliviousness (الغفلة) can only be conceived regarding an existing matter that is being overlooked, and a veil (الغطاء) necessitates something behind it which it covers and hides. Furthermore, the lack of sharpness of sight is only relevant when there is a subtle object of vision (مبصر دقيق) that the sight cannot penetrate.

Among the most absurd views is the claim that the verse is an address from Him, the Exalted, to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), meaning: "You were, before the Message, oblivious of this which We reveal to you, so We removed your veil from you, and your sight today is sharp, perceiving the revelation or seeing the Angel of Revelation to receive the message." This is because the context does not support it, nor does the wording of the verse apply to it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ قَرِينُهُ هذا ما لَدَيَّ عَتِيدٌ "His companion will say, ‘This is what is ready with me’" (50:23). The context is not devoid of an appearance that what is meant by this "companion" (القرين) is the angel entrusted with him. If it is the driver (السائق), then the meaning of his saying: هذا ما لَدَيَّ عَتِيدٌ is: "This human is present with me." If it is the witness (الشهيد), then the meaning is: "This"—pointing to his deeds for which he bears witness—"is what is present and ready with me of his deeds."

It has also been said that what is meant by the companion is the Devil (الشيطان) who accompanied and seduced him. The meaning of his words according to this would be: "This human is the one whose affair I took charge of and whom I possessed; he is present and ready to enter Hell."

His saying, the Exalted: أَلْقِيا فِي جَهَنَّمَ كُلَّ كَفَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ مَنَّاعٍ لِلْخَيْرِ مُعْتَدٍ مُرِيبٍ "[Allah will say,] ‘Cast into hell every stubborn disbeliever, hinderer of good, transgressor, and doubter" (50:24-25). Kaffar (الكفار) is an intensive form of disbeliever (الكفر). The stubborn one (العنيد) is the one who opposes the truth and persists in his obstinacy. The transgressor (المعتدي) is the one who exceeds the limit and oversteps the truth. The doubter (المريب) is the one who doubts or causes others to doubt the matter of the Resurrection.

Between these enumerated attributes, there is a kind of necessary implication (استلزام). For excessive disbelief—by a person's rejection of every truth he faces—produces obstinacy (العناد) against the truth and persistence in it. Persistence in obstinacy necessitates hindering (المنع) most good things, for there is no good except in the truth and from its side. This, in turn, necessitates exiting the boundary of truth into falsehood, and the human's transgression from the boundary of servitude (العبودية) into arrogance and tyranny (الطغيان). It also necessitates making people doubt the true religion they seek.

The address in the verse is from Him, the Exalted, and the manifest context of the verses is that the ones addressed are the two entrusted angels: the driver and the witness. Some have suggested that the address is to two angels from the angels of the Fire and its keepers.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي جَعَلَ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ فَأَلْقِياهُ فِي الْعذابِ الشَّدِيدِ "who set up another god besides Allah; so cast him into the terrible punishment’" (50:26). The shift in mentioning the attribute of polytheism (الشرك) from brevity to elaboration—where He did not say "polytheist" (مشرك) but said: الَّذِي جَعَلَ "who set up..." etc.—is to indicate that this attribute is the greatest of sins and the mother of the crimes he committed, as well as the base attributes enumerated for him, such as disbelief, obstinacy, hindering good, transgression, and causing doubt.

His saying: فَأَلْقِياهُ فِي الْعَذابِ الشَّدِيدِ "so cast him into the terrible punishment" is an emphasis of the preceding command in His saying: أَلْقِيا "Cast...", and it hints at the intensification of the matter due to polytheism; hence He followed it with: فِي الْعَذابِ الشَّدِيدِ.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ قَرِينُهُ رَبَّنا ما أَطْغَيْتُهُ وَلكِنْ كانَ فِي ضَلالٍ بَعِيدٍ "His companion will say, ‘Our Lord! I did not make him rebel, but he was [himself] in far-reaching error’" (50:27). What is meant by this companion is undoubtedly his companion from among the devils. The mention of the companion from the Devil has been repeated in His speech, the Exalted; he is the one who stays close to the human and inspires him with whatever seduction and misguidance he inspires. The Exalted says: وَمَنْ يَعْشُ عَنْ ذِكْرِ الرَّحْمنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُ شَيْطاناً فَهُوَ لَهُ قَرِينٌ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَصُدُّونَهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ حَتَّى إِذا جاءَنا قالَ يا لَيْتَ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ فَبِئْسَ الْقَرِينُ "As for him who blinks at the remembrance of the All-beneficent, We appoint a devil for him, and he is his companion. Indeed they bar them from the way, while they think they are rightly guided. When he comes to Us, he will say, ‘I wish there were between me and you the distance between the east and the west! What an evil companion!’" (43:36-38).

So His saying: قالَ قَرِينُهُ "His companion will say"—meaning his devil who accompanies and seduces him—رَبَّنا "Our Lord!"—he attributes lordship to himself and the human who is his companion because they are in a state of disputation (الاختصام)—ما أَطْغَيْتُهُ "I did not make him rebel," meaning I did not compel him to tyranny, وَلكِنْ كانَ فِي ضَلالٍ بَعِيدٍ "but he was [himself] in far-reaching error," meaning he was prepared and ready to accept what I cast to him, receiving it by his own choice; so I am not responsible for his sin in his rebellion.

The detail of the disputation of the wrongdoers and their associates has already passed in Surah al-Saffat in His saying: احْشُرُوا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا وَأَزْواجَهُمْ "‘Muster the wrongdoers and their mates...’" (37:22) until the end of the verses.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ لا تَخْتَصِمُوا لَدَيَّ وَقَدْ قَدَّمْتُ إِلَيْكُمْ بِالْوَعِيدِ "He will say, ‘Do not dispute in My presence; I had already warned you’" (50:28). The speaker is Allah, the Glorified, addressing them. It is as if it were a single address to the generality of the polytheistic tyrants and their companions, which dissolves into individual addresses to each human and his companion, such as our saying: "Do not dispute (dual) in My presence," etc.

His saying: وَقَدْ قَدَّمْتُ إِلَيْكُمْ بِالْوَعِيدِ "I had already warned you" is a circumstantial clause (حال) from the subject of "Do not dispute," and "the warning" (بِالْوَعِيدِ) is the object of "I had sent forward" (قَدَّمْتُ), with the ba serving as a link.

The meaning is: Do not dispute in My presence, for there is no benefit for you in it after I have conveyed My warning to whoever associates partners [with Me] and does wrong. The warning He sent forward to them is like His saying, the Exalted, to Iblis: اذْهَبْ فَمَنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ جَزاؤُكُمْ جَزاءً مَوْفُوراً "He said, ‘Go! Whoever of them follows you, indeed hell shall be your reward, an ample reward’" (17:63), and His saying: فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكَ وَمِمَّنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ "He said, ‘The truth is—and the truth I say—I will surely fill hell with you and with all of those who follow you’" (38:84-85), or His saying: لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ "‘...I will surely fill hell with jinn and humans, all together’" (32:13).

His saying, the Exalted: ما يُبَدَّلُ الْقَوْلُ لَدَيَّ وَما أَنَا بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ "The word is not altered with Me, and I am not a tyrant to My servants" (50:29). What the context provides is that the verse is an inception (استئناف) serving as an answer to a hypothetical question, as if someone were saying: "Granted that You have sent forward [the warning], why not change it and forgive?" So it is answered with: ما يُبَدَّلُ الْقَوْلُ لَدَيَ "The word is not altered with Me." What is meant by "the word" (القول) is the absolute, final decree (القضاء المحتوم) that Allah has decreed. He has decreed for whoever dies in disbelief that they shall enter Hell, and this applies, according to the context, to the warning Allah gave to Iblis and those who follow him.

It has become clear that the sentence is an inception, and what is meant by the "alteration of the word" (تبديل القول) is the changing of the final decree. "With Me" (لَدَيَ) is connected to the alteration. This is what the context provides. Some have mentioned many other views and possibilities regarding this sentence, the parsing of its individual words, and the meaning of the alteration of the word—views far from understanding that only increase the complexity of the discourse, so we have refrained from mentioning them.

His saying: وَما أَنَا بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ "and I am not a tyrant to My servants" completes the meaning of the previous sentence. That is: My word is not altered, so you are inevitably punished, and I am not unjust to My servants in their punishment according to what I sent forward to them of the warning, because they are deserving of that after the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة).

From another perspective: there is no injustice in requiting them with punishment, for they are only being requited for their deeds which they sent forward; their deeds are returned to them, as is manifest in His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لا تَعْتَذِرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ "O you who have disbelieved! Do not make any excuses today. You are only being requited for what you used to do" (66:7).

The negation of "much injustice" (ظلم كثير) in His saying وَما أَنَا بِظَلَّامٍ does not imply the permissibility of "little injustice." For if He were to do injustice in any part of the requital, it would be "much injustice" due to the multitude of its instances, as the address is to every polytheistic, wrongdoing human along with his companion, and they are many. Thus, He, the Glorified, if He were to do injustice in any part of the requital, would be a "great tyrant" (ظلام).

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ نَقُولُ لِجَهَنَّمَ هَلِ امْتَلَأْتِ وَتَقُولُ هَلْ مِنْ مَزِيدٍ "The day We shall say to hell, ‘Are you full?’ and it will say, ‘Is there any more?’" (50:30). This is an address from Him, the Exalted, to Hell and an answer from it. There has been disagreement regarding the reality of this addressing and speaking. It has been said: The address and answer are in the "language of the state" (لسان الحال). This is refuted by the fact that if it were in the language of the state, it would not be specific to Him, the Exalted; rather, anyone witnessing it in that state could ask it about its fullness, and it would answer: "Is there any more?" Thus, there would be no apparent subtle point (نكتة) for restricting the address to Him, the Exalted.

It has also been said: The reality of the address is to the keepers of Hell, and the answer is from them, even though they are attributed to Hell. This is contrary to the manifest meaning and is not to be adopted except with evidence.

It has also been said: The address and answer are according to their manifest meaning, and there is no evidence indicating its impermissibility. Allah, the Glorified, has informed us of the speaking of hands, feet, skins, and other things. This is the correct view, and it has already passed in the commentary of Surah Fussilat that knowledge and consciousness (العلم والشعور) permeate all existing things.

His saying: هَلِ امْتَلَأْتِ "Are you full?" is a rhetorical question for affirmation (تقريري). Similarly, His saying narrating from it: هَلْ مِنْ مَزِيدٍ "Is there any more?" Perhaps the presentation of this question and answer is to indicate that His power and punishment do not fall short of encompassing the criminals and fulfilling the requital they deserve. The Exalted says: وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ "...and indeed hell besets the faithless" (9:49).

An objection was raised that this contradicts the explicit statement of His saying, the Exalted: لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ "I will surely fill hell..." (32:13). It was answered that "fullness" (الامتلاء) may mean that no part of its levels is devoid of inhabitants, as it is said: "The city is full of its people."

Furthermore, it is possible that this statement from it occurs before all the folk of the Fire have entered it.

It has also been said: The interrogative in His saying: هَلْ مِنْ مَزِيدٍ is for negation (الإنكار), and the meaning is: "There is no more," meaning there is no place for any addition to those criminals who have been cast into me, for I have become full. It would thus be an indication of what He decreed in His saying: لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ "‘...I will surely fill hell with jinn and humans, all together’" (32:13). His saying: هَلِ امْتَلَأْتِ would be in the sense of saying: "The word from Me has come true: I will surely fill Hell," and its saying: هَلْ مِنْ مَزِيدٍ would be an affirmation and confirmation of it.

This view is perhaps supported by His saying earlier: ما يُبَدَّلُ الْقَوْلُ لَدَيَ "The word is not altered with Me," assuming that "the word" refers to His saying: لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأُزْلِفَتِ الْجَنَّةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ "And paradise will be brought near for the God-conscious, not far off" (50:31). This is the beginning of the description of the state of the God-conscious (المتقين) on the Day of Resurrection. Bringing near (الإزلاف) is nearness (التقريب). "Not far off" (غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ), as has been said, is an attribute of a deleted adverbial phrase, the estimation being: "in a place not far off."

The meaning is: Paradise shall be brought near on that day for the God-conscious while being in a place not far off; that is, it is before them, and they face no difficulty in entering it.

His saying, the Exalted: هذا ما تُوعَدُونَ لِكُلِّ أَوَّابٍ حَفِيظٍ "‘This is what you were promised—for every penitent and mindful one" (50:32). The demonstration (الإشارة) refers to the promised reward mentioned previously. The penitent (الأوَّاب) is from awb, meaning return; what is meant is the frequency of returning to Allah through repentance (التوبة) and obedience. The mindful one (الحفيظ) is the one who persists in guarding what Allah has covenanted to him, lest he abandon and lose it. His saying: لِكُلِّ أَوَّابٍ حَفِيظٍ is a predicate after a predicate for "this," or a circumstantial clause.

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ خَشِيَ الرَّحْمنَ بِالْغَيْبِ وَجاءَ بِقَلْبٍ مُنِيبٍ "who fears the All-beneficent in the Unseen and comes with a penitent heart" (50:33). This is an explanation of "every penitent and mindful one." Fearing in the Unseen (الخشية بالغيب) is the fear of Allah's punishment while He is unseen and not visible to him. Turning (الإنابة) is returning. Coming to his Lord with a penitent heart (بِقَلْبٍ مُنِيبٍ) means that he completes his life in penitence, so he comes to his Lord with a heart characterized by penitence.

His saying, the Exalted: ادْخُلُوها بِسَلامٍ ذلِكا يَوْمُ الْخُلُودِ "‘Enter it in peace! This is the day of immortality’" (50:34). This is an address to the God-conscious; that is, it is said to them: "Enter in peace," meaning in safety and security from every disliked thing and evil, or with a greeting of peace (بسلام) from Allah and His angels upon you. His saying: ذلِكَ يَوْمُ الْخُلُودِ "This is the day of immortality" is glad tidings with which they are cheered.

His saying, the Exalted: لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها وَلَدَيْنا مَزِيدٌ "There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more" (50:35). It is possible that "therein" (فِيها) is connected to "they wish" (يشاؤون) or to a deleted word that is a circumstantial clause for the relative pronoun. The estimation would be: "while what they wish is therein," or from the deleted pronoun referring back to the relative pronoun, the estimation being: "what they wish while it is therein." The first is more in accord with the vastness of their honor before Allah, the Glorified.

The result is: The folk of Paradise, while they are in Paradise, possess everything to which their will (المشية) and desire (الإرادة) attach, whatever it may be, without restriction or exception. Thus, they have everything to which desire and will could possibly attach, should they attach.

His saying: وَلَدَيْنا مَزِيدٌ "and with Us there is yet more" means: and they have with Us what is more than that—as the context indicates. Since they have everything to which their will could possibly attach from the goals and objectives within their knowledge, the "more" (المزيد) beyond that is something greater than what their will can attach to, because it is above the perfection that their knowledge can encompass.

It has been said: What is meant by "more" is an increase over what they wish from the same kind of things they desire. Thus, if they wish for sustenance, they are given more of it than they wished, and better and more wonderful. This is as narrated from some: a cloud passes by them and says, "What do you want that I may rain upon you?" and they do not want anything but that it rains it upon them.

This is problematic because it restricts the absolute nature of the discourse without a restrictor. For the manifest meaning of His saying: لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها is that they possess everything they can wish for, not just their possession of what they actually wished for. Thus, the "more" is beyond what their will can possibly attach to.

It has also been said: What is meant is that the good deed is multiplied for them tenfold. This is subject to the same criticism as the previous view.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنا قَبْلَهُمْ مِنْ قَرْنٍ هُمْ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُمْ بَطْشاً فَنَقَّبُوا فِي الْبِلادِ هَلْ مِنْ مَحِيصٍ "How many a generation We have destroyed before them, who were more powerful in might than them! They ransacked the towns: [but] was there any escape?" (50:36). Ransacking (التنقيب) is traveling. Escape (المحيص) is shunning and a place of refuge.

The verse concludes the argument regarding the creation of man, the knowledge of him, and the explanation of his journey to Allah with intimidation and warning (التخويف والإنذار), similar to what occurred at the beginning of the Surah regarding the argument for the Resurrection and its conclusion with intimidation and warning in His saying: كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَأَصْحابُ الرَّسِّ وَثَمُودُ "The people of Noah denied before them, and the inhabitants of Rass and Thamud..." (50:12), etc.

The meaning is: How many a generation We have destroyed before these polytheists who—that is, the people of that generation—were more powerful in might than them—that is, than these polytheists. So they traveled with their might through the lands, conquering them and ruling over them; was there any shunning or refuge from Allah's destruction and His punishment?

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَذِكْرى لِمَنْ كانَ لَهُ قَلْبٌ أَوْ أَلْقَى السَّمْعَ وَهُوَ شَهِيدٌ "There is indeed a reminder in that for him who has a heart, or gives ear and is a witness" (50:37). The heart (القلب) is that by which man reasons, distinguishing truth from falsehood, good from evil, and the beneficial from the harmful. If he does not reason and distinguish, then its existence is like its non-existence, for that which has no effect is the same whether it exists or not. "Giving ear" (إلقاء السمع) is listening, as if the hearing is something cast toward the heard object to reach and perceive it. The witness (الشهيد) is the one who is present and observing.

The meaning is: In the realities We have informed of and the stories of the perished nations We have pointed to, there is indeed a reminder by which he who reasons may remember, thus perceiving the truth and choosing what contains his good and benefit; or he who listens to the truth of the word and is not distracted from it by anything else, while he is a present witness who comprehends what he hears.

The choice between "him who has a heart" and "him who gives ear and is a witness" is because the believer in the truth is one of two men: either a man of intellect (ذو عقل) who can grasp the truth, reflect upon it, see what is true, and thus submit to it; or a man who is not strong enough in reflection to distinguish truth and good, so he must listen to the word and follow it. As for him who has no heart to reason with, nor listens as a witness to what is said to him and cast to him of the Message and warning, he is an obstinate ignorant person with neither heart nor hearing. The Exalted says: وَقالُوا لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ "And they will say, ‘Had we listened or used our reason, we would not have been among the inmates of the Blaze’" (67:10).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ وَما مَسَّنا مِنْ لُغُوبٍ "Certainly We created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days, and no fatigue touched Us" (50:38). Fatigue (اللغوب) is tiredness and exhaustion. The meaning is manifest.

(Hadith Discussion)

In al-Tawhid, with its chain to 'Amr ibn Shimr from Jabir ibn Yazid, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: أَفَعَيِينا بِالْخَلْقِ الْأَوَّلِ بَلْ هُمْ فِي لَبْسٍ مِنْ خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ (50:15). He said: 'O Jabir, the interpretation (تأويل) of that is that when Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, annihilates this creation and this world—and the folk of Paradise settle in Paradise and the folk of the Fire in the Fire—Allah will renew a world other than this world, and renew a creation without male or female who will worship Him and profess His oneness. He will create for them an earth other than this earth to carry them, and a heaven other than this heaven to shade them.

Perhaps you think that Allah only created this single world, or you think that Allah did not create humans other than yourselves? Nay, by Allah, He has created a million worlds and a million Adams—you are in the last of those worlds and [the last of] those Adams.'"

I say: It is narrated in al-Khisal, the first part of the hadith with its chain from Muhammad ibn Muslim from him (peace be upon him). Perhaps what is meant by this being the "interpretation" of the verse is that it is among the things to which it applies.

And from Jawami' al-Jami', from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "كاتب الحسنات على يمين الرجل ـ وكاتب السيئات على شماله ، وصاحب اليمين أمير على صاحب الشمال : فإذا عمل حسنة كتبها صاحب اليمين عشراً ـ وإذا عمل سيئة قال صاحب اليمين لصاحب الشمال : دعه سبع ساعات لعله يسبح أو يستغفر" — "The recorder of good deeds is on the right of the man, and the recorder of evil deeds is on his left. The companion of the right is a commander over the companion of the left: when he performs a good deed, the companion of the right records it tenfold; and when he performs an evil deed, the companion of the right says to the companion of the left: 'Leave him for seven hours, perhaps he will glorify [Allah] or seek forgiveness.'"

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning, and "six hours" is narrated instead of "seven hours."

In Nahj al-Balagha: "وَجاءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَعَها سائِقٌ وَشَهِيدٌ — a driver who drives it to its gathering place, and a witness who testifies against it regarding its deeds."

In al-Majma', Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani narrated with a chain from al-A'mash, who said: "Abu al-Mutawakkil al-Tajir narrated to us from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "When it is the Day of Resurrection, Allah will say to me and to 'Ali: 'Cast into the Fire whoever hated you both, and enter into Paradise whoever loved you both.' And that is His saying: أَلْقِيا فِي جَهَنَّمَ كُلَّ كَفَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ."'"

I say: The Shaykh of the Sect [al-Tusi] narrated it in his Amali with his chain from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi al-Dunya narrated in Dhikr al-Mawt, as did Ibn Abi Hatim and Abu Nu'aym in al-Hilya, from Jabir ibn 'Abdullah, who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying: 'إن ابن آدم لفي غفلة عما خلق له ـ إن الله إذا أراد خلقه قال للملك : اكتب رزقه. اكتب أثره. اكتب أجله شقياً أم سعيداً ـ ثم يرتفع ذلك الملك ويبعث الله ملكاً ـ فيحفظه حتى يدرك ثم يرتفع ذلك الملك. ثم يوكل الله به ملكين يكتبان حسناته وسيئاته ـ فإذا حضره الموت ارتفع ذلك الملكان ـ وجاء ملك الموت ليقبض روحه ـ فإذا أدخل قبره رد الروح في جسده ـ وجاءه ملكا القبر فامتحناه ثم يرتفعان. فإذا قامت الساعة انحط عليه ملك الحسنات ـ وملك السيئات ـ فبسطا كتاباً معقوداً في عنقه ـ ثم حضرا معه واحد سائق وآخر شهيد' — 'Indeed, the son of Adam is in obliviousness of what he was created for. When Allah intends his creation, He says to the angel: "Write his sustenance. Write his traces. Write his term, whether wretched or felicitous." Then that angel ascends, and Allah sends an angel who guards him until he reaches maturity, then that angel ascends. Then Allah entrusts two angels with him who record his good and evil deeds. When death approaches him, those two angels ascend, and the Angel of Death comes to take his soul. When he is placed in his grave, the soul is returned to his body, and the two angels of the grave come to him and test him, then they ascend. When the Hour is established, the angel of good deeds and the angel of evil deeds descend upon him, and they spread a book tied to his neck. Then they are present with him, one a driver and the other a witness.' Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Indeed, before you is a momentous matter that you cannot estimate, so seek help from Allah, the Tremendous.'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ نَقُولُ لِجَهَنَّمَ هَلِ امْتَلَأْتِ وَتَقُولُ هَلْ مِنْ مَزِيدٍ (50:30). He said: "It is an interrogative, because Allah promised the Fire that He would fill it. So the Fire becomes full, then He says to it: 'هَلِ امْتَلَأْتِ?' and it says: 'هَلْ مِنْ مَزِيدٍ?' in the sense of an interrogative, meaning: 'Is there any more [room]?'"

I say: This is based on the interrogative being for negation.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Asma' wa al-Sifat narrated from Anas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'لا تزال جهنم يلقى فيها وتقول : هل من مزيد؟ حتى تضع رب العزة فيها قدمه ـ فينزوي بعضها إلى بعض وتقول : قط قط وعزتك وكرمك. ولا يزال في الجنة فضل ـ حتى ينشئ الله لها خلقاً آخر ـ فيسكنهم في قصور الجنة' — 'Hell will continue to have [people] cast into it while it says: "Is there any more?" until the Lord of Might places His foot in it, whereupon its parts will draw together and it will say: "Enough, enough! By Your might and Your generosity!" And there will continue to be extra space in Paradise until Allah initiates another creation for it and settles them in the palaces of Paradise.'"

I say: The placing of the foot on the Fire and its saying "Enough, enough" (قط قط) is narrated in many reports from the Sunni paths.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها وَلَدَيْنا مَزِيدٌ (50:35). He said: "It is looking toward the mercy of Allah."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bazzar, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Lalika'i in al-Sunna, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Ba'th wa al-Nushur narrated from Anas regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَدَيْنا مَزِيدٌ. He said: "The Lord, the Mighty and Majestic, manifests Himself to them."

In al-Kafi, some of our companions narrated in a marfu' report from Hisham ibn al-Hakam, who said: "Abu al-Hasan Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him) said to me: 'O Hisham, indeed Allah says in His Book: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَذِكْرى لِمَنْ كانَ لَهُ قَلْبٌ (50:37), meaning an intellect (عقل).'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Khatib narrated in his Tarikh from al-'Awwam ibn Hawshab, who said: "I asked Abu Mijlaz about a man who sits and places one of his legs over the other. He said: 'There is no harm in it. Only the Jews disliked that; they claimed that Allah created the heavens and the earth in six days, then He rested on the Sabbath and sat in that posture. So Allah revealed: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ وَما مَسَّنا مِنْ لُغُوبٍ (50:38).'"

I say: This meaning is narrated from al-Dahhak and Qatadah. Al-Mufid also narrated this meaning in Rawdat al-Wa'izin in a weak narration. The origin of dividing the creation of things into six days of the week occurs in the Torah. Although the Quran repeats the mention of the creation of things in six days, it does not mention that these days are the days of the week, nor does it hint at it.

Based on these narrations, those who say the verse is Medinan have relied upon them. However, there is no indication in its refutation of the Jews' claim that it must have been revealed in Medina. Among the Meccan verses are those in which He, the Glorified, addressed the affair of the Jews, as in Surah al-A'raf and others.

Ayahs

فَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ قَبْلَ طُلُوعِ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَقَبْلَ ٱلْغُرُوبِ (٣٩)
So be patient at what they say, and celebrate the praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before the sunset, (39)
وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ وَأَدْبَـٰرَ ٱلسُّجُودِ (٤٠)
and glorify Him through part of the night and after the prostrations. (40)
وَٱسْتَمِعْ يَوْمَ يُنَادِ ٱلْمُنَادِ مِن مَّكَانٍ قَرِيبٍ (٤١)
And be on the alert for the day when the caller calls from a close quarter, (41)
يَوْمَ يَسْمَعُونَ ٱلصَّيْحَةَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلْخُرُوجِ (٤٢)
the day when they hear the Cry in all truth. That is the day of rising [from the dead]. (42)
إِنَّا نَحْنُ نُحْىِۦ وَنُمِيتُ وَإِلَيْنَا ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٤٣)
Indeed it is We who give life and bring death, and toward Us is the [final] destination. (43)
يَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ ٱلْأَرْضُ عَنْهُمْ سِرَاعًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ حَشْرٌ عَلَيْنَا يَسِيرٌ (٤٤)
The day the earth is split open for [disentombing] them, [they will come out] hastening. That mustering is easy for Us [to carry out]. (44)
نَّحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَقُولُونَ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْهِم بِجَبَّارٍ ۖ فَذَكِّرْ بِٱلْقُرْءَانِ مَن يَخَافُ وَعِيدِ (٤٥)
We know well what they say, and you are not there to compel them. So admonish by the Quran those who fear My threat. (45)

(Commentary)

The conclusion of the surah commands the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to be patient with what they say—their accusations of sorcery, madness, and poetry, and their stubborn mockery regarding the Resurrection (المعاد) — the return of all beings to Allah for judgment — and the return to Allah, the Exalted. He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is commanded to remain patient, to worship his Lord by glorifying Him, to anticipate the Resurrection by waiting for the Blast, and to remind through the Quran those who fear Allah in the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge.

His saying, the Exalted: فَاصْبِرْ عَلى ما يَقُولُونَ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ قَبْلَ طُلُوعِ الشَّمْسِ وَقَبْلَ الْغُرُوبِ "So be patient with what they say, and celebrate the praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before sunset" (50:39). This is a derivation from all that preceded regarding the polytheists' denial of the Resurrection, the detailed discourse on the Resurrection and the proofs for it, and the warning to those who deny it and treat the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله as a liar, threatening them with the same fate that befell the deniers of past nations.
